title: Three filled cells and one deliberately absent corner. The filled cells are the valid states explicitly enumerated in the Precept definition. The empty corner is the impossible state — not blocked, not flagged, not crossed out. It simply does not appear in the contract, so it does not exist. Precept's prevention guarantee is not a runtime check but a structural absence: if you didn't write it, it isn't there.
conceptualBridge: The absent cell means Precept makes invalid states structurally absent because the definition only enumerates what exists — the missing corner is not prevented at runtime, it is simply never declared, making the impossible state invisible by design.
decision: Shortlisted: highest-scoring candidate this round (4.75 avg) — the absent corner is the most direct possible image of structural impossibility, the L-shaped silhouette is completely new to the set, and the concept requires no explanation once seen.
